name: Osmium
name_origin: From Greek 'osme' meaning 'smell' or 'odor', referring to the strong, pungent odor of volatile osmium tetroxide (OsO4) formed when the metal is exposed to air.

occurrence.abundance_summary: Osmium is among the rarest stable elements in Earth's crust at about 0.001 ppm. It is found in trace amounts in platinum-group metal (PGM) ores and as native alloys with iridium (osmiridium, iridosmine). It is recovered as a byproduct of nickel and PGM refining, primarily from South Africa and Russia.
states: bound in osmiridium and iridosmine native alloys
trace in nickel and copper sulfide deposits
found in iron meteorites at higher relative levels

compounds.name: Osmium tetroxide
compounds.summary: A pale-yellow volatile crystalline solid used as a fixative and stain in electron microscopy and as a syn-dihydroxylation catalyst in organic chemistry.

compounds.name: Osmium trichloride
compounds.summary: A dark brown solid used as a precursor in osmium organometallic and catalytic chemistry.

compounds.name: Potassium osmate
compounds.summary: A purple water-soluble salt used as a milder, more soluble alternative to OsO4 in dihydroxylation reactions.

compounds.name: Osmium hexafluoride
compounds.summary: A volatile yellow solid noted for its high oxidation state (+6) and aggressive fluorinating behavior.

compounds.name: Triosmium dodecacarbonyl
compounds.summary: A yellow cluster carbonyl that is a benchmark compound in cluster organometallic chemistry.

uses: Hardening agent in platinum and iridium alloys for fountain pen tips and instrument pivots
Electrical contacts and long-wearing instrument bearings
Catalysis in organic synthesis, especially the Sharpless asymmetric dihydroxylation
Tissue fixative and electron-dense stain (OsO4) in electron microscopy and histology
Filaments and tips in some specialized vacuum tubes and field-emission sources
Component in osmium-based dye-sensitized solar cell sensitizers (research)
reactivity_summary: Osmium is the densest naturally occurring element (~22.59 g/cm3) and one of the hardest. The bulk metal is extremely resistant to corrosion at room temperature, but finely divided osmium powder slowly oxidizes in air to volatile, highly toxic, and pungent OsO4. Osmium tetroxide is volatile enough that even bulk metal exposed to oxygen produces detectable amounts. The metal is unattacked by most acids in cold conditions but is dissolved by hot oxidizing acids and aqua regia.
isotopes_summary: Osmium has seven naturally occurring isotopes: 184Os, 186Os, 187Os, 188Os, 189Os, 190Os, and 192Os, with 192Os the most abundant (~40.8%). 184Os and 186Os are alpha-radioactive with half-lives much greater than the age of the universe. The slow 187Re->187Os beta decay underlies the Re-Os geochronometer, used to date sulfide ores, oils, and meteorites.
